text:
This article examines the Jesuit Order as a pioneer of early modern globalization, focusing on their extensive missionary network in the late seventeenth and eighteenth centuries. Central to this study is the "Pimería Alta" region—modern-day Sonora and Arizona—where increasingly large numbers of German-speaking Jesuits followed the path of the pioneer missionary Eusebio Kino. These individuals acted not only as religious figures but also as scientists, cartographers, and ethnographers, bridging the gap between remote colonial outposts and the intellectual centers of Europe. 

The paper highlights the "Welt-Bott," a monumental collection of Jesuit correspondence compiled by Joseph Stöcklein, as a primary source for understanding this global exchange. Through an analysis of these letters and reports, the author demonstrates how the Jesuits established a sophisticated communication system that distributed scientific, geographic, and cultural knowledge across continents. Despite the eventual suppression of the Order in the late eighteenth century and the subsequent fragmenting of historical memory, their written legacy remains a vital record of transcultural encounters and the early systematic exploration of the New World.
